_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Conditionals
summary: Choose which code runs by asking true-or-false questions.
refresher: C++ checks `if`, then any `else if` branches, and finally `else`. Only the first true branch runs. Put braces around each branch so its beginning and end are easy to see.

blocks.body: A conditional lets the program choose. C++ tests the expression inside `if (...)`. If it is true, that block runs. Otherwise, the program tries the next `else if` or uses the final `else`.

Write comparisons that clearly show the rule, such as `score >= 60`. Put the most specific overlapping rule first because only the first true branch runs. Braces make it clear which statements belong to each choice.

blocks.title: Braces do the job indentation does in Python
blocks.body: Python uses indentation to show which statements belong to an `if`. C++ uses braces. Indentation still helps people read C++, but the braces are what the compiler follows.

blocks.caption: A score of 92 satisfies the first condition, so C++ prints A and skips the remaining branches.
Remove the stray semicolon so the block belongs to the condition.

blocks.body: The semicolon in the first version finishes the `if` immediately. The block below it is separate and runs every time. Remove that semicolon so the block runs only when the score is at least sixty.

blocks.title: Do not end the if line with a semicolon
blocks.body: Write `if (condition) {`, not `if (condition); {`. That extra semicolon creates an empty branch and makes the following block run on its own.

blocks.code:
int n=0;
if(n) std::cout<<"yes"; else std::cout<<"no";
blocks.why: Zero converts to false in the condition, so the else branch runs.
Only the first true branch runs, so the broader rule would hide the narrower grade.
